articles.title: This 100-Million-Year-Old Snake Had Legs and a Cheekbone!
articles.summary: Imagine a snake with tiny hind legs and a cheekbone — that's what a fossil from Argentina shows! Scientists used a special scanner to look inside a 100-million-year-old snake called Najash rionegrina. They found it still had a cheekbone (jugal bone), which most snakes today don't have. This changes what we thought about how snakes evolved. Instead of starting as small burrowers, early snakes might have been big-bodied with wide mouths. Cool, right?

articles.title: Why Does Your Heart Race When You're Scared or Excited? Meet Adrenaline!
articles.summary: Have you ever felt your heart pound before a test or when you see your crush? That's adrenaline! This hormone (a chemical messenger in your body) is released when your brain thinks you're in danger. It makes your heart beat faster, helps you breathe quicker, and even slows down digestion. But too much adrenaline over time can be bad for your heart. The good news? Deep breaths, walks, or talking to a friend can help you calm down.

articles.title: NASA Invents a Machine That Makes IV Fluid in Space — No More Expired Bags!
articles.summary: Imagine being on a mission to Mars and needing IV fluid (a special saltwater that helps with dehydration and burns). The problem? Prepacked IV fluid expires after 16 months, and a Mars mission could last three years! So NASA invented IVGEN Mini, a machine that makes IV fluid from space station drinking water. It flew to the ISS on April 11, 2026, and will be tested this spring. It's smaller, lighter, and can produce 1.2 liters per hour — enough to treat emergencies on long space trips.
